items.q: Does adding more RAM let you permanently store more files?
items.correct: No - RAM is volatile working memory; files are stored permanently on secondary storage, so more RAM helps run more programs at once, not store more files.
items.misconception: Yes - more RAM means more space to save documents and photos.
items.random_wrong: Yes - RAM and hard drive space are the same thing.
items.why: Both are measured in GB, so they merge. Separate the desk (RAM, temporary working space) from the filing cabinet (storage, permanent).

items.q: Is the CPU the whole computer case (tower)?
items.correct: No - the CPU is a single chip on the motherboard inside the case; the tower also contains memory, storage, the power supply and other components.
items.misconception: Yes - the box on the desk is called the CPU.
items.random_wrong: Yes - CPU stands for Computer Packaging Unit.
items.why: Everyday speech calls the tower 'the CPU'. In computer science the term means only the processor chip, and exam answers must use it that way.

items.q: Is ROM where the programs you install are stored?
items.correct: No - installed programs go on secondary storage; ROM holds the fixed start-up (boot) instructions written by the manufacturer.
items.misconception: Yes - apps are saved into ROM when you install them.
items.random_wrong: Yes - ROM is just a bigger version of RAM.
items.why: Read-only memory sounds like general storage. Its key property - contents fixed at manufacture - makes it suitable only for firmware, not user software.

items.q: RAM is described as volatile because:
items.correct: Its contents are lost when the power is switched off
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Its contents are lost when the power is switched off. “It catches fire easily” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Programs and files must be kept permanently even when the power is off
items.misconception: RAM is too fast for most programs
items.random_wrong: The CPU cannot work without a DVD drive
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Programs and files must be kept permanently even when the power is off. “RAM is too fast for most programs” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which of these is a function of the operating system?
items.correct: Managing memory, files and running programs
items.why: Correct answer: Managing memory, files and running programs. “Physically cooling the processor” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: A solid-state drive. “The operating system” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A hard drive holds 2 TB. Using 1 TB = 1000 GB, how many gigabytes is that?
items.why: The real figure is 2000 GB. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
